text: Five laparoscopic ports are visible as the surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to mobilize the colon. The lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon are dissected, and the sigmoid colon is gradually mobilized. The rectum is mobilized by dividing the peritoneum on the left side, with care taken to avoid injuring the ureter. A similar dissection is performed on the right side.

The surgeon performs dissection in the presacral fascia, using a harmonic scalpel to dissect the mesorectum, which is taken up with the specimen. A monopolar hook cautery is mentioned as an alternative tool for dissection. The lateral ligaments are carefully dissected and divided with the harmonic scalpel. The dissection progresses into the pelvis, where the inferior mesenteric vein is dissected, clipped with hemolog clips, and divided. The sacral fascia is further dissected caudally, with traction applied to the colon for better visualization. The lateral ligaments are further dissected and divided on both sides.

The surgeon uses a bowel grasper to pull the rectum cranially while further dividing the lateral ligaments with a harmonic scalpel. Dissection proceeds between the bladder and rectum, preserving the seminal vesicles. The inferior mesenteric artery is clipped with hemolog clips and divided, with visible pulsations of the artery.

The surgeon continues caudal dissection, using a harmonic scalpel to dissect and divide the mesocolon. The area of colonic transection is prepared, and an Endo GIA stapler is used to transect the colon.

The surgeon completes the abdominoperineal resection by creating a permanent colostomy from the descending colon. The specimen is extracted through the perineal dissection site. The procedure concludes with multiple liver metastasectomies performed using an open surgical approach.
